title: Colleges you need to avoid...

title: Colleges you need to avoid...

author: @malcorub

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: Correction to the private vs public schools point: private colleges often offer generous financial aid that can make it cheaper to attend than a state school. State schools offer offer almost no institutional aid since they don't have the large endowments of private and are already offering such a reduced tuition to begin with. If you are interested in a non profit accredited private college then go ahead and apply and see how much fin aid they offer you and what the conditions to receive it are. Don't write off private colleges as "too expensive" just by looking at the sticker price of cost of attendance because almost nobody besides the super rich actually pay that amount.
author: @first9299

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: The point about finding a college that supports you is spot on.  Not just for finding a career afterward, but guiding you through your academics for the time you're attending.  Ask current students; how is your academic advisor?  Are they proactive in catching problems before they get too bad?  Regular scheduled meetings during the school year?  Is there tutoring and essay writing assistance located in the library?  Some schools just leave you to fend for yourself; they graduate students who swim and discard those who sink.
author: @bridgecross

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: I'm 26 years old and will be attending a private university in Austin, Texas this fall semester! (I'm really excited after returning back to my home city from new student orientation). I avoided college until mainly because I lacked the structure from not understanding what I'd like to do as a career. Honestly I wasn't aware of the way private universities work (or at least the school where I'm a student at). I just avoided private schools due to the cost. It blows my mind about how opposite my personal case worked out! Summary: 50,000 a year. I received federal grants, 3 school grants, then applied for the 2 small loans my fafsa could offer. My total per semester as a full time on campus student with meal plan is five thousand a semester. That's it. I can more than work with only 10K a year if it means I get to be a part of that campus I've already found my belonging with. My lack of research could have made this happen years earlier ago for me, but once admissions got into contact with me I've gained many big new insights. That is how I found this video because I'm so interesting in learning more about this new concept in my life lol
author: @BrookePainter

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: Good advice. I took up to 19 units once (I wasn't allowed to do more) because I realized I was paying by semester and why wouldn't I take advantage of that and get the most out my school. That also meant I was qualified to graduate a semester early if I wanted (but decided to use the last semester to study abroad). Another point to think about is that sometimes a more "respected" school will have a better alumni network and more resources. I didn't qualify for any government aid because I worked through community college. I got into all the state schools I applied to but was offered either loans only or a little scholarship money. However, the one school that would have been the most "respectable" (at the time) covered my whole tuition and enough for books. (I only borrowed a little for living expenses). This was mainly because they had more donors and could help more students. Apply everywhere and often the financial aid package comes with or shortly after the acceptance letter, it can help you make your choice sometimes.
author: @melissaclemons8922

title: Colleges you need to avoid...
body: my cousin got into NYU and Brown but it was so expensive. So instead he chose Kings College London, UK which ranked 35th globally and cost 70% less. If anyone is interested in going to a good University i would suggest not just looking in USA but also Europe. The major advantage is that the undergraduate degree is just 3 years and someone can get masters degree as well for far less cost.

title: THE PROS & CONS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF MIAMI !
body:
Pros:
- Genuinely great programs (Music, Architecture, Marine Bio, Medicine, etc.)
- Beautiful campus and if you want to have fun in college, you will surely have fun here at the U.
- Great facilities but you should expect that from an expensive private school.

Cons:
- EXPENSIVE. I think there's no reason to go here unless you get full financial aid or if this is the best option you could take and you can comfortably afford it.
- The alumni network isn't all that compared to similar ranking schools.
- The housing situation is DIRE.

It's a great university I just think it's overpriced for what you get.
author: @twistedfantasyy

title: Ranking EVERY Florida College #college #university #collegelife #fsu #uf
body:
UCF’s biggest plus is it’s in Orlando. You’re going to naturally have a good night life and plenty to do. As someone from South Florida it was the perfect distance from family to have some separation while also being close enough that going home for breaks and holidays wasn’t too much of a hassle.

Biggest downside is the massive student body. Parking is a mess and if you’re not a social butterfly it can be intimidating.

title: Day in the Life of 27 Year Old Multi-Millionaire Prop Trader
body:
Smartest thing he said: 'one internal mechanism for me that held me back from putting risk in the wrong places was that on those B and C trades I would naturally feel nervous putting on too much risk and that internal sign was the key indicator to me as to the quality of that trade'.

This is a something that can only be learned through experience. Not everyone will be good at trading, and there are a million different ways to make money in the market. But if you find a way that you are good at, and then you make some mistakes, you will eventually come to realize that the difference between the trades that made and lost you money is more easily differentiated in your gut than on the chart.
author: @theflippestside

title: Day in the Life of 27 Year Old Multi-Millionaire Prop Trader

title: Day in the Life of 27 Year Old Multi-Millionaire Prop Trader
body:
There’s something quietly powerful about watching a trader move through his day with that mix of intensity and composure. What struck me most wasn’t the size of the risk limits or the fast execution moments, but the emotional undercurrent he never quite says out loud — the discipline to hold steady, the self-awareness to step back, and the honesty of admitting when the market humbles you.
The part about the million-dollar drawdown lingered with me. Not because of the number, but because of the mindset: scaling down, rebuilding from smaller green days, and choosing clarity over ego. It’s rare to see someone talk about the slow, uncomfortable parts of trading without dramatizing them. In a world obsessed with highlights, this felt strangely grounding.
I also found it interesting how he views rest not as a break, but as a strategic tool — the idea that mental capital is just as valuable as financial capital. That perspective says more about the reality of trading than any chart ever could.
It left me thinking: in a profession built on speed and precision, is the real edge actually hidden in these quiet, disciplined moments that no one sees?
author: @FinGateway

title: Day in the Life of 27 Year Old Multi-Millionaire Prop Trader
body: prop traders can reach exciting levels of success (multiple six- and seven-figure years), the journey to get there involves risk management discipline, self-awareness, adaptability, and a strong support network. His biggest lesson is recognizing that not trading—or trading with small size—during lower-probability setups is just as important to long-term success as swinging big on your best trades.
author: @high-reshallucinations

author: @gloryofGodfillstheearth

title: University of Miami Campus Tour
body: Ahhh my beautiful campus... I miss my UM days... c/o 97.... its much harder to get in these days I hear.  When I got into UM in 92' I had a 820 SAT combined score and a 3.4 weighted gpa 3.13 un-weighted.  Thank God I got in when I did.  I hear you need crazy SAT's and GPA's now.  I was very lucky.  My Recommendations were very strong and I was very active in the extra carriculars at my high school.  I remember I was admitted into a special program that made the kids with lower SAT's and GPA's (but showed promise) have to attend one of the summer sessions and take freshman english and an elective or you can take english and math.  It was great so when school started, i was done with the first semester of English and Math since that was all i did in the summer and i got A's in both because there were only 16 of us in the program. We got a lot of one on one time with the professor and the TA's... and they made it so much fun.  I don't know if they offer that program anymore but it was the best thing for me because if it weren't for that, I would have never gotten in.  Once the semester started, our group was a head of the rest of the kids coming in for the first time because we were at UM all summer.  The thing is, if you don't pass both courses; you will be asked to not join us in the fall.  I don't remember anyone that did not coming back because we all work so hard to stay in.  once the summer was over, we were all full fledged UM students.  The school always kept track of us through out the years and every now and again to check on us and see how we were doing but for the most part, we all were fully fledged UM students.  UM Baby!!!! I hope this helps.  Never herts to ask if a program like this is still in place.  Its not just for athletes, African American Students, Latino or White students.  there were kids from all backgrounds in our program.  It was the most fun.  Good luck next generation.  Keeping you in my prayers... Cause all the stories you have heard about the U are true.  You will have the most fun you will ever have at a University.  and my degree from UM opens doors for me everyday.  every job I have ever gotten was because they were impressed with the fact that i went to the U.   Any questions for me, let me know...
author: @prskyguy

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: I graduated from UCF in 1990. Back then, the school was small with an undergrad enrollment around 25K. It has completely changed now. It's too big in my opinion.
author: @DesertScorpionKSA

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: I go to FGCU and I absolutely LOVE the school. I wish he talked more about the water and nature because that is what the whole campus revolves around. And that waterfront is literally the best because you can rent out boats, canoes, kayaks, paddle boards, etc. as well as a beautiful resort style pool and aquatic center. The place truly looks like a resort and it blows me away every day that I get to study there.
author: @nadiafalcone9665

title: Touring Every Florida College So You Don't Have To | (UF UM UCF FAU ETC)
body: Love being an Owl alumni! It’s a completely different campus than when I was there, there was no engineering building, they hadn’t built the gym and new pool until my senior year, and we still had to drive to some random high school stadium 30 minutes away because the stadium hadn’t been built. The rapid development of that campus has been amazing to watch, and I love getting tickets that have access to the full service bar IN THE STADIUM! How awesome is that?!
author: @Alex-cz4fk
